questions.question: Explain the difference between Computer Organization and Computer Architecture.
keyPoints: Computer Organization deals with physical components and interconnections
Computer Architecture defines functional behavior and logical structure
Organization includes hardware units, bus structures, memory technology
Architecture includes instruction set, addressing modes, data formats

questions.question: What are the main components of the Central Processing Unit (CPU)? Explain the function of each.
keyPoints: Control Unit - directs operation and issues control signals
ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) - performs arithmetic and logical operations
Registers - small fast storage locations for temporary data
Cache memory - fast memory for frequently accessed data

questions.question: Describe the Fetch-Decode-Execute cycle in brief.
keyPoints: Fetch: PC address sent to MAR, instruction fetched to IR, PC incremented
Decode: Instruction in IR is decoded to determine operation
Execute: ALU performs the operation using data from registers
Store: Result stored back to memory or register

questions.question: What is the stored-program concept? Who proposed it?
keyPoints: Both instructions and data are stored in the same memory
Programs can be treated as data
Proposed by John von Neumann
Forms basis of most modern computer architecture

questions.question: Explain the purpose and function of different types of buses in a computer system.
keyPoints: Data bus: transfers actual data between components
Address bus: carries memory addresses for data access
Control bus: carries control signals like read/write, interrupt
System bus combines all three for CPU-Memory-I/O communication

questions.question: What are the different types of memory in a computer system? Explain.
keyPoints: Primary Memory: RAM (volatile, fast access), ROM (non-volatile)
Secondary Memory: HDD, SSD, optical drives (permanent storage)
Cache Memory: fast, small, sits between CPU and main memory
Registers: fastest, inside CPU, minimum capacity

questions.question: Explain the function of the following registers: PC, IR, MAR, MDR, ACC.
keyPoints: PC (Program Counter): holds address of next instruction
IR (Instruction Register): holds current instruction being executed
MAR (Memory Address Register): holds address for memory operations
MDR (Memory Data Register): holds data being transferred to/from memory
ACC (Accumulator): stores intermediate arithmetic results

questions.question: Draw a neat labeled diagram of the basic structure of a computer system and explain the data flow between its components.
keyPoints: Draw Input, Output, Memory, CPU blocks with connections
Show bus system connecting components
Explain data flow: Input -> Memory -> CPU -> Memory -> Output
Explain address flow and control signal flow
Mention stored-program concept with program in memory

questions.question: Explain the working of a microprogrammed control unit with the help of a diagram. Also mention its advantages over a hardwired control unit.
keyPoints: Diagram showing Control Store (ROM), Microsequencer, Control Unit outputs
Microinstructions stored in control store
Each machine instruction implemented by sequence of microinstructions
Advantages: easier to modify, less complex wiring, more flexible, easier to design
Disadvantages: slower execution than hardwired

questions.question: Discuss the basic concepts of RISC and CISC architectures. Compare their characteristics.
keyPoints: RISC: Reduced Instruction Set Computer - simple, few instructions, fixed length
CISC: Complex Instruction Set Computer - many instructions, variable length
RISC: load-store architecture, more registers, pipelining easy
CISC: memory-to-memory instructions, microcode control
Compare: performance, hardware complexity, compiler complexity

questions.question: Explain the concept of instruction pipelining and its advantages. Also discuss pipeline hazards.
keyPoints: Pipelining: overlapping execution of multiple instructions
Stages: Fetch, Decode, Execute, Memory, Write-back
Advantages: increased throughput, better utilization of resources
Hazards: Structural (hardware conflict), Data (dependency), Control (branch)
Solutions: forwarding, stalling, branch prediction
